story:
Smoky skeins of blue sorcery coil above a wooden worktable as an Aélkyn apprentice hurls every healing charm he knows at his closest friend. Sweat beads on his round face; his slim fists hammer the table until the paper lamps he summoned flare orange and bright. Nothing takes. The boy’s gaze flicks to a looming wall of books, as if the tomes themselves have betrayed him. His friend lies still, eyes blank to the rafters, body in tatters. Despair hardens into a promise: I’ll bring you back.

Beyond those everyday spellbooks sleeps a velvety purple volume—massive, ancient, and heavy with outlawed power. Years ago his father told him never to touch it. He was five then. Six years later, fear and love bloom into reckless certainty. If healing fails, only necromancy remains. The thought chills him, yet he remembers how the tome once raised hives on his skin, how even its dust felt alive. He murmurs a countercharm, steels his nerves, and drags the book to the table as if it might bite.

He smells like the flooded graveyard he once trudged with his father for ingredients. The irony stings. No time for irony. He palms the cover, rides out the numbing thrum, and lives. No hives. No marks. His heart pounds like a Dojí war-drum, but he turns pages anyway, hunting through elder tongues—Eólkyn, Télkyn, his own Aélkyn—for what polite studies never taught: how to raise the dead. On page two hundred eleven, crimson ink names a spell: Dúm Ras Daae Ún, On Raising the Once-Dead. He can read the title, not the author; the script bites like frost. He lifts his arms to begin.

“What do you think you’re accomplishing?” comes a voice. The door slams. Bright day cuts around a tall silhouette. For a heartbeat he fears he’s loosed a daemon. Then the shape resolves into his father: white hair streaked purple and black, emerald-banded tail, amber ear-jewels, a beard the boy once tried to climb. No laughter now. His father takes in the wrecked body, the scattered books, the forbidden tome, and the frightened child. He sighs, and removes the purple book with ease, stowing it inside his robe like a docile thing.

“I wanted to bring him back,” the boy chokes. “Please, can you save him? I’ll do anything!” His father’s look is stern but not cruel. Necromancy, he says, is for dead flesh—real flesh. With human creations come human remedies. The boy flinches, unsure. “So he’s… dead?” “No more than you or I,” his father replies, voice low and even. “Take him to the Redwood Market. Find a human who can patch him.”

“GO!” snaps the command, and the boy bolts, clutching his friend. As he runs, cotton spills from a ripped belly, and a loose button eye drops, bumping across the floor to rest at his father’s boot. The truth sits plain: the cherished best friend is a stuffed bear, a human-made comfort torn by hard use, not a soul lost to death. The father kneels, studies the black button, and lets it fall. “Humans and their toys,” he mutters, gathering the borrowed books. The lesson lingers like fading sorcery: not every wound asks for forbidden power; sometimes, the right magic is a needle, thread, and the hands that made the thing in the first place.
